topic: 0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Globalization,,Globalization — definition,"Process which led to the appearance of a truly global economy; Convergence of cultures and tastes; Economic: inflow and outflow of goods and services."

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Divergence,,Divergence — definition,"Process which led some areas of the world to become much richer and more powerful than others; Not just in levels but also in rate of change."

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Divergence,,Divergence — indicators,"Indicators: Per-capita GDP; Urbanization rates; Living standards (health levels and life expectancy), e.g. 20th century divergence in health going hand-in-hand with divergence in economic indicators."
0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Divergence,,Divergence — why it matters,"Underlines why some areas failed and what led to areas’ success."

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Inequality,,Inequality — definition,"Differences in income and wealth within and between countries and world regions; Condition of disparity in distribution of income and economic assets."
0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Inequality,,Inequality — core question,"Question about reduction of global inequality leading to increase in national inequality."

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Cultural Globalization,,Cultural globalization — Britannica (Watson) definition,"A phenomenon by which the experience of everyday life, as influenced by the diffusion of commodities and ideas, reflects a standardization of cultural expressions around the world. Propelled by the efficiency or appeal of wireless communications, electronic commerce, popular culture, and international travel, globalization has been seen as a trend toward homogeneity that will eventually make human experience everywhere essentially the same. This appears, however, to be an overstatement of the phenomenon. Although homogenizing influences do indeed exist, they are far from creating anything akin to a single world culture. (Watson, Cultural Globalization, Encyclopedia Britannica 2016)"

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Economic Globalization,,Economic globalization — UNDESA (Gao Shangquan) definition,"Economic globalization refers to the increasing interdependence of world economies as a result of the growing scale of cross-border trade of commodities and services, flow of international capital and wide and rapid spread of technologies. It reflects the continuing expansion and mutual integration of market frontiers, and is an irreversible trend for the economic development in the whole world at the turn of the millennium. (Gao Shangquan, U.N. Department of Economic and Social Affairs paper, n.1/2000)"

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Economic Globalization,,Economic globalization — central aspects (Britannica/Volle)","Central aspects: interconnection; intensification; time-space distanciation (conditions that allow time and space to be organized in a manner that connects presence and absence); supraterritoriality; time-space compression; action at a distance; accelerating interdependence."

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Economic Globalization,,Economic globalization — deterritorialization + definition (Britannica/Volle),"Modern analysts also conceive of globalization as a long-term process of deterritorialization—that is, of social activities (economic, political, and cultural) occurring without regard for geographic location. Thus, globalization can be defined as the stretching of economic, political, and social relationships in space and time."

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Economic Globalization,,Economic globalization — examples (Britannica/Volle),"A manufacturer assembling a product for a distant market, a country submitting to international law, and a language adopting a foreign loanword are all examples of globalization."

0. Introductory Concepts & Definitions,Economic Globalization,,Economic globalization — historical examples (Britannica/Volle),"History is filled with such occurrences: Chinese artisans once wove silk bound for the Roman Empire; kingdoms in western Europe honoured dictates of the Roman Catholic Church; and English adopted many Norman French words in the centuries after the Battle of Hastings. (A. Volle, “Globalization”, Encyclopedia Britannica, 2024)"
1. Origins of Divergence,1.1 Timeline,,Timeline — Paleolithic Age (dates + label),"Paleolithic Age (2.6 Mio – 10.000 BCE) Hunter-Gatherers
1. Origins of Divergence,1.1 Timeline,,Timeline — Neolithic Age (dates + label),"Neolithic Age (10.000 – 2.000 BCE) Agrarian Revolution"
1. Origins of Divergence,1.1 Timeline,,Timeline — Bronze Age (dates + label),"Bronze Age (3.300 – 1.200 BCE) Urban Revolution"
1. Origins of Divergence,1.1 Timeline,,Timeline — Iron Age (dates + label),"Iron Age (1.200 BCE – 500 AD) Great Empires & Iron"
1. Origins of Divergence,1.1 Timeline,,Timeline — Medieval Period (dates + label),"Medieval Period (500 – 1500) Proto-Globalization/Discovery"
1. Origins of Divergence,1.1 Timeline,,Timeline — Early Modernism (dates + label),"Early Modernism (1500 – 1800) Divergence/Pre-Industrial"
1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.1 Childe – Classical Theory / Oasis Theory,Childe — core hypothesis,"Hypothesis: Humans discovered agriculture via Oasis Theory."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.1 Childe – Classical Theory / Oasis Theory,Childe — mechanism (climate → oasis → domestication),"Climate change at end of Pleistocene led to drier climate and droughts; this forced humans, plants, and animals to live in closer proximity at areas termed as oasis; this closer proximity led to closer relationship and association, i.e. domestication."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.1 Childe – Classical Theory / Oasis Theory,Childe — population effect,"Then population grows because mortality decreases due to better nutrition."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.2 Cohen – Demographical Explanation,Cohen — core claim,"Discovery of agriculture not through climate/oasis and not like an instance of time for discovery; hypothesis: agriculture was the way to adapt to demographics."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.2 Cohen – Demographical Explanation,Cohen — global saturation → food crisis chain,"End of Pleistocene, humans occupied all available “prime” land on Earth → global saturation → no more room for H&G groups to move when their populations grew → increase in population density → reduction in available wild resources per person → food crisis."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.2 Cohen – Demographical Explanation,Cohen — solution despite worse work,"To survive, humans had to find a way to get more calories out of same amount of land → domesticating plants and animals increased the productivity per hectare and thereby increased the carrying capacity of their territory, even if it meant more back-breaking labour."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.2 Cohen – Demographical Explanation,Cohen — “life got worse” parallel to Pomeranz,"Similar argumentation as Pomeranz: life did not get better but actually got worse — less physical activity (sedentary lifestyle); higher proximity to animals; higher population density → higher risk of catching infectious diseases; evidence: smaller skeletons of farmers vs. hunter-gatherers."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.2 Cohen – Demographical Explanation,Cohen — reproduction logic for population growth,"Overall lower cost for reproduction (i.e. did not have to move around with baby) and higher return for children such that mortality increases but fertility increases by even more, explaining the population growth post-Neolithic Revolution."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – History’s Have & Have Nots / To Farm and Not to Farm,Diamond — uneven emergence and why it matters,"Why food production emerged unevenly across regions; which is the ultimate cause of later differences in population density, states, and technology, as well as conquest."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – History’s Have & Have Nots / To Farm and Not to Farm,Diamond — “fertile vs fertile” + avoid determinism,"Question is not fertile vs. infertile land but fertile vs. fertile; plus avoid being geographically deterministic."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – History’s Have & Have Nots / To Farm and Not to Farm,Diamond — Eurasia’s early “package” → Guns/Germs/Steel advantage,"Explaining why Eurasia accumulated powerful “packages” early (food production → states/technology/germs) that by the time of transoceanic contact, Eurasian societies already had Guns, Germs, and Steel advantages."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – History’s Have & Have Nots / To Farm and Not to Farm,Diamond — why North America becomes modern leader,"Northern America becoming modern leader largely because it was populated/colonized by people that came from Eurasia’s high-tech states; they inherited that high-tech accumulated package which came in handy with their huge pool of land and resources."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Domestication Timeline,Domestication timeline — dates/regions,"~8.500 BCE: Plants — Fertile Crescent (Southwest Asia); ~8.000 BCE: Animals — Fertile Crescent (Southwest Asia); ~7.500 BCE: Crops & animals — China; 6.000–3.500 BCE: Crops & animals — Europe (by diffusion, not independent); 2.500–1.500 BCE: Crops — Eastern US (first local founders)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 5 Discussion Regions,Diamond — “primary centers” definition,"“Primary centers” = independent origins of food production: regions where agriculture/herding arose de novo (people domesticated local plants/animals before any domesticated package arrived from elsewhere)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 5 Discussion Regions,Diamond — compelling primary centers,"Strong evidence for compelling centres: Southwest Asia / Fertile Crescent; China; Mesoamerica (central/southern Mexico + adjacent Central America); Andes + possibly adjacent Amazonia; Eastern United States."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 5 Discussion Regions,Diamond — Fertile Crescent special role,"Fertile Crescent: exceptionally rich early set of domesticable plants and animals as well as an early start date for farming that diffuses outward."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 5 Discussion Regions,Diamond — candidate centers + why uncertain,"Candidate centres (possible independent): Sahel; Tropical West Africa; New Guinea; Ethiopia. They’re “candidates” because there’s uncertainty whether food production was truly independent or triggered by arrivals from elsewhere (e.g., cattle possibly from Fertile Crescent into Sahel; Southwest Asian crops possibly arriving before/local domestication in Ethiopia; earliest crops in New Guinea not securely identified)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 5 Discussion Regions,Diamond — what needs explanation (where farming didn’t appear until modern times),"It’s unsurprising farming didn’t arise in places still ecologically hard today (Arctic, deserts). What “cries out for explanation” is why farming failed to appear until modern times in places that are excellent agricultural zones today, e.g.: California/Pacific states; Argentine pampas; SW & SE Australia; Cape region (South Africa)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 5 Discussion Regions,Diamond — adoption vs replacement,"Agricultural development either by adoption without replacement (gradual incorporation) or abrupt replacement by incoming farmers."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 5 Discussion Regions,Diamond — key claim leading to Ch. 6,"Even if a region looks “fertile,” food production depends on whether it had a domesticable package available locally (plants/animals worth domesticating) and (Ch. 6) whether pressures/conditions made adopting farming advantageous (population dynamics, wild resource abundance, etc.)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 6 Why adopt Farming when worse?,Diamond — why adopt if early farming worse?,"Early farming made lives often worse (nutrition, workload, activity, disease) → so why would you? Underline that they did not choose into progress."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 6 Why adopt Farming when worse?,Diamond — farming vs H&G as competing strategies,"Interpretation as comparing “lifestyles” either hunter-gatherer or farming — competing strategies: full-package farming; piece-meal (some farming, some H&G); reject & full H&G."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.3 Diamond – Ch. 6 Why adopt Farming when worse?,Diamond — five factors/drivers of farming,"Five factors/drivers of farming: (I) decline of wild foods; (II) increased availability of domesticable wild plants; (III) cumulative technology for harvesting, processing & storing; (IV) positive feedback between population and food production; (V) competition and displacement of H&G (denser food-producing groups outcompete H&G; choice is get displaced or adopt)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.4 Characteristics of Agrarian Societies,Characteristics of agrarian societies — negative outcomes,"Negative outcomes: poorer diet; higher prevalence of disease; increased amount of working hours per-capita."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.4 Characteristics of Agrarian Societies,Characteristics of agrarian societies — positive outcomes (toward urban revolution),"Positive outcomes leading to urban revolution: better organized and more ambitious societies could develop; division of labour; more complex social structures → accumulation of knowledge."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.2 Neolithic Revolution,1.2.4 Characteristics of Agrarian Societies,Characteristics of agrarian societies — writing,"Discovery of writing allowing passing on knowledge across generations."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.1 Diamond – Advantages of Some Crops over Others,Diamond — crop package superiority claim,"Domesticable species available in Asia are superior to those available in the Americas and Oceania; due to the East-West axis they were later exported to Europe; geography matters."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.1 Diamond – Advantages of Some Crops over Others,Diamond — Americas disadvantaged by late arrival,"Americas further disadvantaged by later arrival/late human colonization."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.2 2 Major Advantages of Crops & Animals,Two major advantages — overview,"(I) More domesticable species available; (II) Some of those better (more productive, too)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.2 2 Major Advantages of Crops & Animals,Plants — wheat/rice vs maize/potato + domestication difficulty,"Plants: wheat and rice are nutritionally superior to maize and potato; maize was difficult to domesticate — wild species are deeply different from current domesticated ones; domestication took millennia for maize and only few centuries for wheat."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.2 2 Major Advantages of Crops & Animals,Animals — Eurasian large mammals vs llama,"Animals: cows and horses are more versatile, stronger, and better suited for hard work than llama; in the Americas llama was the only domesticable mammal of large size available compared to 13 species available in Eurasia."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.3 Geography,Geography — East-West axis advantages,"East-West axis → migration: same climatic belt → agriculture can spread; travel → innovation; Americas bottleneck; other barriers (desert and mountains)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.3 Geography,Geography — time of arrival (peopling the Americas),"Humans originating from Africa; 14,000 years ago North America; ~2,000 years to make full trip from Alaska to Patagonia."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.3 Geography,Diamond Ch. 10 “Tilted Axes” — core claim,"Importance of latitude → migration & diffusion; axis determines how easily crops and livestock thrived and technology diffused; Eurasia on East-West axis (similar latitude, climate & day length); Americas & Africa on North-South axis forces crops and animals to adapt to different climates, day lengths, and diseases."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.3 Geography,Diamond — why faster diffusion?,"Why faster diffusion? Latitude is the primary determinant of growing conditions; domesticated bundles move easier between regions with similar latitudes."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.3 Geography,Diamond — diffusion examples (Eurasia vs Americas),"Evidence/examples: Southwest Asia → Europe; Philippines → Polynesia; versus Mexico → US; Andes → northward."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.3 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better for Agriculture,1.3.3 Geography,Diamond — “fail to farm” puzzle + barriers/spillovers,"Fail to farm in Australia, California, South Africa’s Cape although farming nearby; geography blocking passage with key animals; axis effects also have spillover to other innovations (e.g. wheel/alphabet)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.1 Diamond – Ch. 6 Farming Outcome,Agriculture → state capacity (overview),"Agriculture leading to state capacity: food surplus & storage → some people can be fed without farming full-time; non-farmers can pursue other tasks (soldiers, administrators, priests, artisans); overall state organizational capacity increases as well as technology."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.1 Diamond – Ch. 6 Farming Outcome,Surplus → population density → taxation/organization feedback,"Supporting higher population density → intensification feedback loop; larger and more settled societies are easier to tax and to organize; overall key for states and armies."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.1 Diamond – Ch. 6 Farming Outcome,Stratification & centralisation as state capacity,"Stratification & centralisation: management of land, irrigation, storage & redistribution tends to create hierarchies; institutions that can mobilise labor and resources develop state capacity."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.1 Diamond – Ch. 6 Farming Outcome,Domestic animals as power multiplier + germs,"Domestic animals = power multiplier: farm animals used for transportation and traction increasing productivity but also for military; zoonotic germs — long exposure to herd animals → infectious diseases such that dense farming societies develop more immunity to crowd diseases."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.2 Bronze Age — Full Effect of Advantages,Urban revolution — cities and first states (ca. 3000 BCE),"From ca. 3,000 BCE cities emerge in Europe and Asia; considerable increases in complexity of economies and societies due to ability of city to impose organization of activities of large surrounding territory; first cities → first states."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.2 Bronze Age — Full Effect of Advantages,Urban revolution — tribute/tax and new tasks,"Ability to raise tributes (tax) and use those tributes/concentration of resources to accomplish new and more complex tasks."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.2 Bronze Age — Full Effect of Advantages,Urbanization as proto-divergence,"Mostly localized process (Asia and Europe); urbanization as proto-divergence of Eurasia from the rest."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.4 Urban Revolution,1.4.3 Advantages & Steps of Urbanization,Steps of urbanization (I–VI),"I. Tributary state (able to collect tributes from subjects); II. Coordination of labour and accumulation of resources; III. More ambitious societies; IV. Better irrigation (applying water on land); V. Higher productivity per land → food production above subsistence → surplus → can focus on other problems than food; VI. Changes in property rights, inheritance system (descendance), marriage & other social institutions (e.g., where women move to, what in return, what as insurance…)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.1 Diamond – Political Fragmentation,Political fragmentation — Europe vs Americas (15th c.),"End of 15th century / time of Columbus’ expeditions: Americas — only 2 empires able to mobilize large amounts of resources; Europe — competition between cities/states → fragmentation as comparative advantage of Europe over the Americas (but what about Asia?)."
1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.1 Diamond – Political Fragmentation,Eurasia — many states,"Eurasia: large amount of more or less developed states."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.2 Goody – Fundamental Similarities,Goody — counterargument to “European miracle”,"Counterargument (like Pomeranz) to European miracle: Eurasia fundamental similarities in institutions and cultural traits."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.2 Goody – Fundamental Similarities,Goody — rejects “Europe uniquely better” claims,"Opposed to institutional and cultural explanations for great divergence that claim: (i) Europe had uniquely “better” culture (rationality, individualism, work ethic, etc.), or (ii) Europe had uniquely “better” institutions (property rights, rule of law, “modern” family, etc.), while “the East” was structurally different in deep, long-run ways."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.2 Goody – Fundamental Similarities,Goody — why relevant here,"In our context, this reasoning holds for comparing Eurasia to the rest; focusing on: urban revolution and the development of tributary states; intensification of agricultural production in Eurasia through improved irrigation resulted in class divisions."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Surplus → division of labour → class division,"Agricultural surplus → leads to division of labor → class division."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Class division + devolution + marriage strategies,"Class division based on devolution (giving power to), which in turn is based on specific family institutions and complex marriage strategies to avoid dispersion of patrimonies."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Property-heavy agrarian systems — inheritance + women as conduits,"In intensive, property-heavy agrarian systems: inheritance rules become central; women become part of the property/inheritance system in specific ways — property transmission is not only a male lineage affair; women can be conduits of property (e.g., dowry, inheritance shares, marriage transfers), even if their rights are constrained or mediated."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Key assets list,"Key assets: land; herds; irrigated plots; stored grains; workshops."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Household as economic unit + conjugal vs descent groups,"When key assets are privately or quasi-privately controlled: household becomes an economic unit; the conjugal family’s interests can come into tension with wider descent groups (clans and lineages)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Meaning of “families more independent from wider kin groups”,"Weaker domination by lineage elders over property decisions; more emphasis on the household estate (patrimony) as the unit to protect/manage; more deliberate control of marriage and fertility because they affect inheritance → property devolution reshapes family structure."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Fragmentation problem + why marriage strategies emerge,"Each generation risks fragmenting the estate (splitting land among heirs, transferring wealth out via daughters’ marriages, competing claims among kin) → societies develop “marriage strategies” to manage the flow of property."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Marriage strategies — examples across Eurasia,"Dowry systems; endogamy/cousin marriage; rules about inheritance distribution paired with marriage norms; strong parental control/arranged marriage (marriage as economic strategy)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Why not (or late) in Sub-Saharan Africa?,"Cities never as large; cities not able to organise activities of vast areas (environment partly responsible, too); methods of cultivation not as productive reducing importance; less diversified society such that hereditary transmission of rights on land was never really that important (nowhere near the importance as in Eurasia)."

1. Origins of Divergence,1.5 Proto-Divergence / Eurasia better by Institutions,1.5.3 Class Divisions & Family Institutions,Sub-Saharan Africa — outcomes + pathogens note,"Outcomes: African family institutions deeply different from Eurasian; pursuit of complex marriage strategies not that important. Also, higher rate of pathogens point: you need some density to have epidemics; less urban space in Africa."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.1 Definition,Great Divergence — definition,"the process which led Western Europe and some “European offshoots” in the New World to experience relatively quick rates of economic growth and to emerge as the most powerful and wealthy civilization of the world → in terms of Economic Growth and Power (i.e. Ability to impose Authority over others). European Offshoots being the Areas where Population with European origins dominant characterized by institutions and cultural traits with a predominantly European character."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.2 Theories for Beginning,Theories for beginning — 3 timing buckets,"I Earliest Origins (Black Death, 1347–1352): Clark & parts of Broadberry; Mechanism: Mortality → Factor Prices → Wages / Malthusian relaxation. II Early Origins (Early Modern, 1500–1700): 16th: New Maritime Routes; 17th: Scientific Revolution. III Late Origins (Industrial Revolution, late 18th/early 19th): Not earlier than 1750; some also underline that not just recent but also just short-lived."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.2 Theories for Beginning,Earliest origins — Clark/Broadberry mechanism,"Earliest origins (Black Death, 1347–1352): Clark & parts of Broadberry; Mechanism: Mortality → Factor Prices → Wages / Malthusian relaxation."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.2 Theories for Beginning,Early origins — Early Modern triggers,"Early origins (Early Modern, 1500–1700): 16th: New maritime routes; 17th: Scientific Revolution."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.2 Theories for Beginning,Late origins — Industrial Revolution timing claim,"Late origins (Industrial Revolution, late 18th/early 19th): not earlier than 1750; some also underline that not just recent but also just short-lived."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.3 Measurement & Problems,Measurement — urbanization proxy logic (Acemoglu et al. 2005),"Urbanization: Acemoglu et al. (2005) – The rise of Europe. Assuming non-agricultural activity in urban areas/cities, then urbanization rates measure total surplus in agriculture beyond subsistence and thereby proxy for LT-development. Urbanization ↔ Non-agricultural share ↔ Agricultural surplus."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.3 Measurement & Problems,Measurement — GDP per capita reconstructions issues (Broadberry 2013),"GDP per-capita reconstructions: Broadberry (2013) – Accounting for the Great Divergence. Uncertainty + unit-of-analysis issue (countries vs city-regions)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.3 Measurement & Problems,Measurement — real wages: definition + key problems,"Real wages: diminishing return to labor; wage = marginal product/revenue; problem: whose wages?/who is “typical” worker?; Kumon (2019) – Wages and Incomes in Japan versus Europe, 1600–1870."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.3 Measurement & Problems,Real wages — implication for productivity,"So if real wages increase, then productivity/output must increase, too."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.3 Measurement & Problems,Pomeranz — sketch notes,"Overcome original constraints; certain sets of conditions which are similar for …; if they followed that path then they would have but at war → face constraints; Japan major restauration: abolish shogunate; only asian country to transition to industrial economy."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Demographic bucket — core mechanism (Clark/Broadberry),"Population shocks and demographic regimes change the land–labor ratio and therefore real wages, incentives, and the ability to accumulate. In a Malthusian economy, productivity gains tend to be eaten up by population growth, keeping living standards near subsistence."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Demographic bucket — Black Death shift,"A major mortality shock (Black Death) can shift Europe to a higher land/labor ratio: higher real wages; more meat/land-intensive consumption; potentially more human capital investment and urban growth."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Demographic bucket — Clark persistence claim,"In Clark’s version, what matters is not just the initial shock but persistent high mortality (plague becomes endemic), preventing population from fully re-absorbing gains → wages stay higher longer, helping divergence."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Demographic bucket — evidence used,"What it tends to use as evidence: real wage series (especially post-Black Death wage increases; long-run wage divergence); sometimes urbanization as a proxy for surplus and structural change; Broadberry-style reconstructions can be used to argue for earlier European lead (with uncertainty)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Demographic bucket — limits/critiques,"Limits / common critiques: region selection (Europe is heterogeneous; which “Europe” is diverging?); mechanism gap (higher wages don’t automatically imply industrialization—why doesn’t every high-wage region industrialize?); comparability (different baskets, labor types, and representativeness — “whose wage?”)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Geographic-Geopolitical bucket — framing,"This bucket mixes three related but distinct logics. Introduce it as: constraints + competition + external resources/power."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Pomeranz — constraints and “escape” (coal + colonies): core mechanism,"Advanced Eurasian regions faced similar preindustrial constraints (land, biomass energy, diminishing returns). Britain escapes via coal and the New World. The key is not “Europe was always better,” but that industrialization required breaking an ecological/energy bottleneck. Coal substitutes for land-based energy (wood/charcoal). Colonies / Atlantic trade substitute for land by importing resource-intensive goods (land, timber, cotton, calories), easing pressure in the core."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Pomeranz — evidence style,"Evidence style: resource scarcity indicators (wood prices, deforestation), energy constraints; trade flows that function as “virtual land/energy”; wage patterns consistent with a late takeoff."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Pomeranz — limits/critiques,"Limits / critiques: coal existence isn’t enough—why Britain specifically, why then?; how large was the colonial contribution relative to domestic changes?; debate over whether Asia’s “core regions” were truly comparable."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Diamond — geography/diffusion/state competition: core mechanism,"Deep geography shapes agriculture, population density, state formation, technology diffusion, and disease exposure—creating unequal starting positions. East–west axis (Eurasia) supports diffusion; north–south axes hinder it. Domesticable plants/animals and resulting immunity advantages. Often used to explain why some regions become conquerors in the long run."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Diamond — evidence style + limits,"Evidence style: very long-run comparative geography/biogeography; patterns of diffusion and domestication. Limits/critiques (in divergence debates): explains very long-run inequality well, but is less precise about timing and location of the 18th–19th century break (Britain vs Yangzi Delta, etc.); risks being too “structural” to explain short-run institutional choices."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Wallerstein — world-system hierarchy: core mechanism,"Europe’s early advantage becomes self-reinforcing through a global system with a core extracting surplus from peripheries via unequal exchange and coercion. Center–periphery relations shape specialization patterns: core — higher-value production, finance, military/political control; periphery — raw materials, coerced labor, dependence. The system “locks in” relative underdevelopment."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Wallerstein — evidence style + limits,"Evidence style: colonial trade structures, coercive institutions, monopoly companies; persistent global inequality patterns and path dependence. Limits/critiques: can over-attribute change to international trade elites and underplay internal developments in Asia/Europe (Pomeranz’s critique); hard to quantify causality (does the system cause divergence, or reflect it?)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Institutional bucket — core mechanism,"Rules, organizations, and political structures shape incentives for innovation, investment, market integration, and state capacity—making sustained growth more likely in some places than others."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Institutional bucket — III.a Knowledge & innovation institutions (Needham problem),"Puzzle: if China was highly advanced in science/tech, why does Europe pull ahead after ~1500? Proposed mechanism: European institutions (universities, autonomous towns, bourgeois incentives) create a stronger pipeline from knowledge → commercialization → sustained innovation. Often paired with Europe’s political fragmentation/competition (overlaps with geography/geopolitics)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Institutional bucket — III.b Market-supporting and state institutions,"Secure property rights, contract enforcement, financial markets, representative constraints on rulers, etc. Predicts earlier commercialization, urbanization, and eventual industrial capacity. Broadberry sometimes sits here when arguing that institutional/structural differences show up in earlier GDP/urbanization patterns."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Institutional bucket — III.c “World-economy” institutions (Braudel; sometimes Wallerstein),"Braudel’s “world-economy” lens: semi-autonomous economic zones with their own centers; capitalism as layered above markets (networks, credit, privileged actors). Emphasis on how organized commerce, finance, and state-linked actors shape the trajectory of expansion."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Institutional bucket — evidence style,"Evidence style: institutional histories (charters, universities, trading towns); proxies: urbanization, market integration, interest rates, fiscal capacity, patenting/science later."

2. The Great Divergence,2.1 Great Divergence & Measurement (Problems),2.1.4 Overview of Explanations/Causal Taxonomy,Institutional bucket — limits/critiques,"Limits / critiques: institutions are often hard to measure and easy to label after the fact; risk of “selection on the outcome” (calling what succeeded “better institutions”); needs to explain why similar commercial institutions in parts of Asia did not yield the same takeoff (Pomeranz’s challenge)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.2 Timeline,,Timeline — Polycentric Eurasia (ca. 750–350 BCE): headline + why it matters,"Period: Polycentric Eurasia; Dates: ca. 750–350 BCE; Headline: Multiple “world-economies”; What matters: separate, largely autonomous regional systems (Braudel/Wallerstein framing). No single integrated world economy yet; “centers” exist but within regional spaces."

2. The Great Divergence,2.2 Timeline,,Timeline — Imperial integration (ca. 200 BCE–500 CE): headline + why it matters,"Period: Imperial integration (still limited); Dates: ca. 200 BCE–500 CE; Headline: Empires + long-distance trade; What matters: Roman/Han era: some coherence (Mediterranean; Silk Road links), but trade is mostly high value-to-weight goods → integration is real but not mass."

2. The Great Divergence,2.2 Timeline,,Timeline — Eurasian reconnection shock (1206–1350s): headline + why it matters,"Period: Eurasian reconnection shock; Dates: 1206–1350s; Headline: Pax Mongolica + pathogens; What matters: Mongol communications network restores/enhances Silk Road connectivity; ideas/goods move more easily. Pathogens move too → sets up Black Death transmission."

2. The Great Divergence,2.2 Timeline,,Timeline — Demographic regime break (1347–1500): headline + why it matters,"Period: Demographic regime break (early-origin candidate); Dates: 1347–1500; Headline: Black Death & high-wage Europe (Clark); What matters: Europe’s mortality shock + recurrent plague can sustain higher real wages and loosen Malthusian pressure (demographic causal story). This is the anchor for the “earliest origins” view."

2. The Great Divergence,2.2 Timeline,,Timeline — Great Opening / proto-globalization (1434–1550): headline + why it matters,"Period: The Great Opening (proto-globalization); Dates: 1434–1550; Headline: Oceanic routes + “opening of closed worlds”; What matters: Portuguese breakthroughs (caravel; Cape routes) + Columbus (1492) = expansion of navigable space. Key point: psychological/cultural transformation matters as much as trade volume (Chaunu). This is a backbone for early-origins stories."

2. The Great Divergence,2.2 Timeline,,Timeline — Global system construction (1550–1700): headline + why it matters,"Period: Global system construction; Dates: 1550–1700; Headline: Colonial extraction + center/periphery begins; What matters: integration of Atlantic + Indian Ocean into a broader system; early colonial empires. Wallerstein: early small Western advantage becomes self-reinforcing via hierarchical exchange (center/semi-periphery/periphery)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,,Eurasia before opening — multiple world-economies (ca. 750–350 BCE),"In Eurasia we had multiple world-economies (ca. 750–350 BC): not one big world-economy; they were independent and almost entirely separated."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.1 Concepts: “World-Economy” and “World-System”,Braudel — world economy vs world-economy,"Fernand Braudel: One global/world economy vs. a world-economy. The world economy is an expression applied to the whole world. A world-economy only concerns a fragment of the world, an economically autonomous section."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.1 Concepts: “World-Economy” and “World-System”,Wallerstein — world-system theory (world-economies)","Immanuel Wallerstein – World System Theory: each world-economy related to a specific geographic space, which is also interconnected, hierarchically-organized, and self-sufficient economic space. Each world-economy is almost entirely separated from the others. Each has its own center/periphery."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.2 Eurasia before the Opening: “almost apart”,Roman Mediterranean coherence — “Apart”,"Great empires such as the Roman Empire create greater coherence, structure, and connection across the Mediterranean world economy; but still we had self-sufficient economic areas organized in their own way."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.2 Eurasia before the Opening: “almost apart”,Silk Road connectivity — “Almost” (core points),"Main connection between Roman Empire and Han Dynasty was the Silk Road (206 BC – 220 AD); little volume traded of only most precious/high value-to-weight goods."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.2 Eurasia before the Opening: “almost apart”,Silk Road connectivity — examples of goods traded,"Silk/tea/spices/porcelain vs gold/silver/wool/linen; also linking North India; even caravan routes crossing Sahara desert and reaching Central Africa."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.2 Eurasia before the Opening: “almost apart”,Decline of Silk Road — reasons,"Empire shifts; conflict & security issues; later rising relative cost vs maritime."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.3 Antecedent shock: Mongol Empire (revives routes + spreads pathogens),Mongol Empire — key effect on Eurasian connectivity,"Mongol Empire did something similar as Roman Empire: it allowed the revival of the Silk Road by again unifying a vast amount of region of the Silk Road guaranteeing safety and trade conditions."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.3 Antecedent shock: Mongol Empire (revives routes + spreads pathogens),Mongol timeline — 1206 (Genghis Khan),"1206: Temüjin proclaimed Genghis Khan; unification of all different Mongol tribes; expansion to Pax Mongolica (Central Asia, China, and Eastern Europe)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.3 Antecedent shock: Mongol Empire (revives routes + spreads pathogens),Mongol timeline — 1271 (Marco Polo),"1271: Marco Polo reaching court of Kublai Khan."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.3 Antecedent shock: Mongol Empire (revives routes + spreads pathogens),Mongol timeline — 1279 (Kublai Khan / Yuan + extent),"1279: Kublai Khan — China → Yuan Dynasty; Asia: Tibet, Korea, and large part of Indian subcontinent; Europe: Russia, Poland, and Hungary → constant threat to entire continent → largest contiguous empire in human history."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.3 Antecedent shock: Mongol Empire (revives routes + spreads pathogens),Pax Mongolica — why it mattered,"Importance in history of Eurasia for strengthening exchange of goods; revival of Silk Road (Pax Mongolica) — relative stability and safety to areas they controlled."

2. The Great Divergence,2.3 Great Opening / Proto-Globalization,2.3.3 Antecedent shock: Mongol Empire (revives routes + spreads pathogens),Pathogens — 1346 Black Death route note,"1346: (spread of pathogens) Black Death to Black Sea from Himalaya."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Why Age of Discovery matters even if trade volumes are tiny — core claim,"The importance of ancient long-range routes connecting different parts of Eurasia and Africa should not be underestimated. However, very few people had the opportunity to follow those paths, and very few goods were valuable enough to make such a long-range trade convenient. As a consequence, the process leading to the opening of new trade routes and to the integration of ever larger areas in a kind of constantly-expanding world economy, has a crucial importance."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Silk Road and “Global” trade — what’s the key point beyond trade volume?,"Not just trade especially because just few people could go and just few goods were valuable enough to make long-range trade convenient — but also/more importantly transformation of psychology/culture."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Transformation of psychology/culture — what is the motivation?,"Knowing that it is possible to reach China; not only economic importance but also for how you look at the world → there is a world beyond ours."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Pierre Chaunu — “opening of the closed worlds” quote/claim,"The «opening of the closed worlds» which occurred between the mid-15th and the mid-16th century marks a breakpoint in human history and the start of a process of change of the utmost importance."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Pierre Chaunu — proto-globalization phase,"The opening of the closed worlds marks a first phase of proto-globalization."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Pierre Chaunu — what changes once naval routes are possible?,"Once possible to trade and travel through naval routes everything changes."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Chaunu — even if trade volumes are tiny (1/10,000 in 1550), what is systemic?,"Even if trade volumes are tiny (1/10,000 in 1550), the change is systemic: route feasibility (naval travel becomes repeatable); strategic incentives (cutting out middlemen, state competition); worldview shift (psychology/culture → motivation → continued exploration)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.1 Why it matters even if trade volumes are tiny,Stylized pyramid of circles — distribution of output across exchange spheres,"~90% of output is consumed within the household/farm (autoconsumption, subsistence). ~9% is exchanged locally (within the village/immediate locality). ~1% reaches a wider local network (nearby villages/local market towns). ~0.1% reaches urban markets (towns/cities as regional nodes). ~0.01% reaches national/inter-regional trade (long-distance within the polity/region). ~0.001% reaches international/intercontinental trade."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.2 Italian merchant republics,Italian merchant republics — what they did (galleys + routes),"Start with Italian galley that commercialized the trade routes between Mediterranean and the Nordic/Baltic Sea."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.2 Italian merchant republics,Italian merchant republics — motivations,"Knowledge of other worlds existing gives motivation, added to the motivation to cut out middle men, and then finding areas along the way. (See Little Divergence)"

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Portuguese breakthrough — 5-part mechanism (a–e),"(a) Technological synthesis: cog robustness + Mediterranean maneuverability → caravel. (b) Political sponsorship: crown/Henry the Navigator; explicit R&D request. (c) Psychological threshold: Cape Bojador as “proof + unknown winds” → confidence. (d) Persistence/path dependence: “Africa shorter” belief sustains effort. (e) Comparative hook: “Cape of Tempests” / why Europe not China."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Continuity with Italian expansion — but key limits,"Continuity with earlier expansion of sea trade routes spearheaded by Italian merchant republics (Venice, Genoa…), which from the 13th century sent naval commercial expeditions beyond the Mediterranean. Although the Italians managed to open sea trade routes with the Baltic, to the south they were unable to go beyond the port city of Safi in Morocco. Also the expedition westward, in the open Atlantic, led by the Vivaldi brothers in 1291 was a failure. The Vivaldi brothers used galleys. The Portuguese had a great technical advantage in having developed the caravel."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Portuguese success — why closely related to naval technology?,"The success of the Portuguese is closely related to the progress made in naval technology, by combining northern and Mediterranean models to produce something new: a ship able to face the Atlantic waves, like the cog, but quick and manoeuvrable like the Mediterranean ships."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Caravel — where/when/how developed and by whom?,"The caravel was developed in Portuguese shipyards in the mid-15th century, following an explicit request by the crown (through Henry «the Navigator», a son of King John I of Portugal) and with the specific aim of allowing for the exploration of the Atlantic."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Tech superiority as explanation — and a caveat,"Technological and scientific superiority is one of the possible explanations of the Great Divergence — although also this view has been challenged: see later, Needham."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Cape Bojador — double significance,"The rounding of Cape Bojador had a double significance: technical (it proved that the new ships were up to the task) and psychological, as beyond the cape, the conditions of the winds were entirely unknown and a considerable degree of self-confidence was needed to advance holding some hope of being able to make return."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Caravel as psychological achievement + “Africa shorter” belief,"Caravel not only technological achievement but also psychological as moving beyond means that world is open. By direct intervention of royal family along shores of Africa. One big mistake: thinking that Africa was much shorter (faster to circumnavigate). Decades of failing but that “mistake” was not ignorance but made them hopeful, i.e. keep going — again underlining the psychological side of the story."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Galley vs cog vs caravel — key characteristics,"Galley good for …; Cog: round shape & high walls; good for withstanding high waves in oceanic conditions but difficult to manoeuver. Caravel: (the hybrid) combines Atlantic robustness with manoeuvrability."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.3 Portuguese breakthrough: Technology & State,Cape of Tempests — comparative hook (Europe vs China) notes,"Cape of Tempest: why Europe but not China? why not China but Europe? They did get to it but moment to get to West Africa face worse conditions while European start with worse conditions."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.4 Portuguese chronology (1434–1534): route-building sequence,1434 — Cape Bojador (Gil Eanes) significance,"1434: the Portuguese Gil Eanes is the first European explorer to double Cape Bojador, in western Morocco. This is the starting point of a long process of exploration and commercial expansion spearheaded by the Portuguese, who tried to reach India by sea circumnavigating Africa."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.4 Portuguese chronology (1434–1534): route-building sequence,~40 years later — Gulf of Guinea,"About 40 years later, the Portuguese reached for the first time the Gulf of Guinea — just about half the trip to the southern tip of Africa."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.4 Portuguese chronology (1434–1534): route-building sequence,1488 — Bartolomé Diaz (Cape of Good Hope),"1488: Bartolomé Diaz returns to Lisbon, after having doubled for the first time the Cape of Good Hope, the southern extreme of Africa."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.4 Portuguese chronology (1434–1534): route-building sequence,1498 — Vasco da Gama reaches Calicut,"1498: Vasco da Gama reaches Calicut in India. It is the fulfillment of a dream that the Portuguese had been following for almost a century."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.4 Portuguese chronology (1434–1534): route-building sequence,1510 / 1513 / 1543 — later route milestones,"1510: Malacca Peninsula in Malaysia. 1513: China. 1543: Japan."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.5 1488-1492-1498,1488–1492–1498 — the three anchors,"1488: Bartolomeu Dias — Cape of Good Hope (rounding Africa is possible). 1492: Columbus — Americas. 1498: Vasco da Gama — Calicut."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.5 1488-1492-1498,Why Portugal rejects Columbus + why Spain funds him,"Portuguese wanting to cut out Venetians from pepper trade; (Italian) Columbus looking for funding: goes to King of Portugal → gets rejection because can do ourselves plus “nearly” done with circumnavigating Africa; goes to Spanish King. POV of Spanish: Portuguese are going to make it sooner or later."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.5 1488-1492-1498,1492 — what else happens? (notes list),"1492: Columbus Americas; end of Reconquista (Kingdom of Granada); PT: South America sugar cane; Genova IT: sugar production in Iberian Peninsula."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.6 Integration of pre-existing networks (Indian Ocean + trans-Saharan + Silk Road),Portuguese control of Indian Ocean routes — Calicut 1498,"When Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in 1498, ancient naval trade routes already existed, connecting different parts of the Arabic Sea and of the Indian Ocean. Thanks to their superior weapons and vessels, the Portuguese quickly took control of them."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.6 Integration of pre-existing networks (Indian Ocean + trans-Saharan + Silk Road),Taking over trade without military confrontation — China–Japan example,"Something similar happened elsewhere, not necessarily through military confrontation — for example, the Portuguese could take over trade between China and Japan because the Japanese had been forbidden access to the Chinese ports (see video The Portuguese in Japan)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.6 Integration of pre-existing networks (Indian Ocean + trans-Saharan + Silk Road),Proto-global system integrates pre-existing systems,"The new global economic system which started to develop during the so-called «Age of Explorations» integrated the pre-existing systems (the Silk Road; the trans-Saharan routes; the Arabic sea routes in the Indian Ocean…) into something much broader and more closely connected."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.6 Integration of pre-existing networks (Indian Ocean + trans-Saharan + Silk Road),Chaunu — tiny shares (1/10,000) shouldn’t trick us,"The part of the total product which moved along the new routes was tiny — according to Chaunu, maybe 1/10,000 at 1550. These small numbers should not trick us: they had huge consequences — they had the strength to force open previously closed «worlds», to shatter ancient equilibria, to change deeply the way in which human being thought about the world and the role they played in it."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.6 Integration of pre-existing networks (Indian Ocean + trans-Saharan + Silk Road),Village approach — the 6 shares (repeat),"90% auto-consumption/subsistence; 9% local exchange (within the village, immediate locality); 1% wider local network (nearby villages, local market towns); 0.1% reaching urban markets (towns/cities as regional nodes); 0.01% reaching national/inter-regional trade (long-distance within the polity/region); 0.001% international/intercontinental trade."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.6 Integration of pre-existing networks (Indian Ocean + trans-Saharan + Silk Road),Why epochal? (1434–1550 proto-globalization),"This was an epochal change — as it created the path that we have been following until now. In this sense, we can state that in the period 1434–1550 a kind of «proto-globalization» took place."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.7 ... and the Spaniards sail West,Portugal turns down Columbus — why?,"When the Italian explorer Cristoforo Colombo asked the king of Portugal to finance an expedition westwards aimed at reaching the Indies, he was turned down — as the Portuguese had almost achieved the same objective by taking a different route."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.7 ... and the Spaniards sail West,Spain funds Columbus — why?,"The king and queen of Spain, Isabelle of Castille and Ferdinand of Aragon, were more interested — as they were also interested in gaining commercial independence from the Portuguese."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.7 ... and the Spaniards sail West,Spanish conquest milestones (1492 / 1518–20 / 1522–23),"1492: Colombo reaches San Salvador in the Bahamas archipelago. 1518–20: Hernán Cortés conquers Mexico and puts an end to the Aztec Empire. 1522–23: Francisco Pizarro conquers the Inca Empire."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.7 ... and the Spaniards sail West,Diamond’s triad — why advanced American states didn’t stand a chance,"The superiority of the Europeans in «guns, germs and steel», as Diamond has it, is obvious. The most advanced states of America did not have a chance."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.8 Diamond — Conquest & Biological Exchange “Guns, Germs, and Steel”,Diamond — core claim on empires + why they lose,"Diamond describes only 2 large empires when first in contact with South America → they do not stand a chance due to multiple factors. Europeans bring guns, germs, and steel."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.8 Diamond — Conquest & Biological Exchange “Guns, Germs, and Steel”,Virgin soil populations — what does it mean?,"Pathogens meet “virgin soil population”: no immunity, no antibodies, no knowledge (treatment); native population particularly susceptible."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.8 Diamond — Conquest & Biological Exchange “Guns, Germs, and Steel”,Columbian Exchange — pathogens list + note,"“Columbian Exchange”: plague, small pox, flu/cold, halmatone (there is a problem with pathogens creating respiratory problems because symptoms of them are so similar); syphilis for which Europeans are virgins."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.8 Diamond — Conquest & Biological Exchange “Guns, Germs, and Steel”,Spanish conquest — timing + Aztec weakness + collaborators,"Got lucky in terms of timing, too. When arrived Aztec Empire just done with terrible civil war as the Aztecs were highly aggressive and extractive to their subjects in surrounding area. Not only did they find Aztecs to be weakened but also found many collaborators. (Not Diamond) but politics matters too: conquest success depends on local alliances + timing (civil war) + extractive empire resentment."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.8 Diamond — Conquest & Biological Exchange “Guns, Germs, and Steel”,Italian Wars & syphilis (1494–1559) — transmission story,"Italian Wars 1494–1559: King of France claim over King of Naples. Prostitutes to soldiers ratio very high and already here high infection of syphilis making it spread across Italy and Europe (French/Italian disease). Those that got infected were killed in nasty way. But within a century, the pathogen lost its potency."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.8 Diamond — Conquest & Biological Exchange “Guns, Germs, and Steel”,Pathogen evolution intuition + Ebola example,"In general any pathogen does not want to kill host quickly, i.e. does not want to be too efficient, such as Ebola."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.8 Diamond — Conquest & Biological Exchange “Guns, Germs, and Steel”,Knowledge/prohibition note (preservation + Church),"But also knowledge got better starting with preservation but also Church with prohibition."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.9 Colonization & Slave Trade: Coercion and Labor,Colonization & slave trade — coercion definition,"Coercion is not just violence, it is a production system that scales extraction."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.9 Colonization & Slave Trade: Coercion and Labor,Slave trade — established by whom?,"Slave trade first established by Portuguese and then later by others, too."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.9 Colonization & Slave Trade: Coercion and Labor,Why coercion could be established (notes),"Could establish by natives’ lack of warfare; population density and disease spread in Europe → immunity."

2. The Great Divergence,2.4 Age of Discovery (mid-15th to mid-16th): Mechanisms & Key Episodes,2.4.9 Colonization & Slave Trade: Coercion and Labor,Coercion + monopoly trade institutions — implication,"Coercion + monopoly trade institutions → help create persistent center-periphery asymmetries."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),,Proto-globalization → hierarchical world-system (1550–1700) — core claim,"After 1434–1550, oceanic routes do not just add trade; they reconfigure power: control of chokepoints/ports → control of commerce; violence + monopoly + legal privileges → ability to set terms of trade; integration of older networks (Indian Ocean / trans-Saharan / Silk Road) into a broader system increasingly shaped by European naval power."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),,Link to Great Divergence logic (Wallerstein-style path dependence),"A small initial advantage can become self-reinforcing once hierarchy and enforcement exist (Wallerstein-style path dependence)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.1 Empires around 1600,Empires around 1600 — different styles of empire (Portugal vs Spain vs newcomers),"Different styles of empire (useful distinction for later): Portugal — coastal forts/enclaves + control of sea lanes and trading nodes (Goa, etc.). Spain — larger territorial conquest in the Americas + extraction and settlement. Dutch/English/French (“newcomers”) — later entry, often using privileged companies and naval competition to challenge Iberian dominance."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.1 Empires around 1600,Geopolitical implication — fragmentation → multiple bidders,"Europe’s political fragmentation → multiple “bidders” for expansion → persistent competition, experimentation, and escalation at sea."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.1 Empires around 1600,Economic implication — empires as enforcement systems,"Empires are not only geography; they are systems of extraction + protection + enforcement."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.1 Empires around 1600,Portuguese in Africa and Asia — two anchors (1482/1509),"The Portuguese in Africa and Asia: 1482 Elmina Castle in Ghana; 1509 enclave of Goa."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.1 Empires around 1600,Empire map sketch (Spain/Portugal/France), "Spain: Central America & West South America. Portugal: Eastern South America & Africa. France: North America."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.2 Terms of Trade + Inequality Logic,Terms of trade + inequality — core idea,"Core idea: hierarchy becoming self-reinforcing — once a center can enforce trade conditions (naval power, forts, monopolies), exchange can become systematically unequal."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.2 Terms of Trade + Inequality Logic,Wallerstein mechanism chain — step-by-step,"Emergence of core with military/financial capacity → creation of peripheries supplying land-/labor-intensive goods (raw materials, plantation products) → unequal exchange + coercion → surplus flows to the core → reinforcement: core invests in ships/arms/finance → stronger enforcement → more favorable trade."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.2 Terms of Trade + Inequality Logic,“Conditions of trade keep you at the level that you are” — meaning,"Specialization patterns get locked in (who exports what; who captures markups). “And if you rebel then superior power intervenes” (enforcement clause)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.2 Terms of Trade + Inequality Logic,Why terms of trade matter for divergence,"Can support capital accumulation, state revenues, and learning-by-doing in shipping/war/finance in the core; can weaken industrial paths elsewhere by keeping peripheries focused on primary goods and coerced labor."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Privileged trading company — definition + why it matters,"In the seventeenth and the eighteenth century, commercial and colonial expansion — especially that of “newcomers” such as the Dutch, the English and the French — was favoured by a crucial institutional innovation: the privileged trading company."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Privileged trading companies — key privileges + state-like powers,"These companies were given specific privileges, such as the exclusive right to use certain routes or to trade in specific goods. The major ones were also given some state-like powers, such as the power to maintain a fleet and army and to govern the bases and territories they obtained outside Europe."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Who benefits + deniability clause,"They allowed many bold traders to make a fortune, and also represented an excellent new investment opportunity for the wealthy (including for rich nobles who could become shareholders without dirtying their hands with ‘bourgeois’ activities). To some degree, they allowed a kind of deniability: they were not state institutions, and (to a point) what happened in the colonies remained in the colonies…"

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Risk + finance function,"Pool risks of long-distance trade (shipwreck, war, piracy, disease); turn colonial ventures into investable assets (shares), widening the investor base."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Monopoly rents + enforcement function,"Monopoly → ability to set prices/markups; private violence (company troops) → enforce contracts and suppress rivals locally."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,State-building feedback loop,"Company profits/taxes support naval capacity; naval capacity supports companies → feedback loop."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Example VOC — founding + monopoly term,"Dutch East India Company (Vereenigde Oostindische Compagnie, VOC): established in 1602; given a 21-year monopoly to trade in Asia."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,VOC — shares + scale + profits,"Shares in the company could be bought and negotiated; it was by far the largest European trading company in Asia; it reaped huge profits from its trades in spices — and in slaves…"

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,VOC — “prototype of a hybrid” definition,"VOC = prototype of a hybrid: private capital + state-backed sovereignty → efficient at building and defending overseas networks."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Micro-case: Jan Pieterszoon Coen — who/when and mortality risk,"Jan Pieterszoon Coen (1587–1629): born to a modest family of traders; joined his first VOC expedition in 1607, aged 20 — at that time, mortality for those who joined such trips was about 50%."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Coen — position and strategy against rivals,"In 1617, he became governor-general of the East Indies — and fought to keep off European rivals from the Asian trade."

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Coen — Banda Islands 1621 + nutmeg markup,"He was also very harsh with some native populations — such as in the case of the attack on the Banda Islands (the place of production of the nutmeg) in 1621. Note that nutmeg was sold in Europe at 840 times the price at the source…"

2. The Great Divergence,2.5 Proto-Globalization to Hierarchical World-System (1550–1700),2.5.3 Institutional Tool of Expansion: Privileged Trading Companies,Wallerstein vs Pomeranz — what matters about overseas access,"For Wallerstein, these institutions help build durable hierarchy; for Pomeranz, overseas access matters especially insofar as it relaxes land/energy constraints (not just merchant profits)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),,Why Europe, not China? — the key framing question,"The question is not only “who had the technology?” but “who had incentives, institutions, and political conditions to persist with maritime expansion?”"

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),,Europe vs China — key contrast (fragmentation vs single decision center),"Key contrast: Europe = multiple competing states; China = one imperial decision center (lower “option value” if the court says no)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Portuguese in Japan by “random” — firearms + after war,"Portuguese in Japan by “random”: provide firearms for civil/Shogunate war; after war done: ban Portuguese then replace by Dutch."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Japan–China–Portugal triangle (silver/piracy/intermediation) — notes,"Japanese taking over China: goods easily taken from China in 16th century → silver → pirates Japanese; so Portuguese could take over (not entirely peacefully): they took what and when they wanted because technology (firearms) so much better; military fighting — first against locals, then against other Europeans."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Why China not by sea — ships vs politics + “Chinese Columbus”,"Why China not by sea: wrong ships, too; but mostly via politics. “Chinese Columbus” could only go to 1 emperor, i.e. not political fragmentation."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,External threat / innovation note (China) — bullets,"Credible external threats (guns and steel): China did not have it → LT disadvantage for China; making China potentially less innovative, too; civil war too, i.e. welcoming innovation."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Japan closure — shogunate logic,"Shogunate decides to close itself wanting to end trouble until Major Restoration."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,China stops imperial exploration — bullets,"China stop imperial exploration as major achievements have been done; emperor fight pirates; change focus to Oceania to take power there."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Geography add-on — “cut-off Americas”,"Another reason why not China is geography being cut-off Americas, too."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Overview — fragmentation vs centralized veto,"Political fragmentation (Europe): competition among states raises payoff to innovation and exploration; if one rejects (Portugal), another funds (Spain) → “portfolio of sponsors.” Centralized veto (China): exploration depends on court priorities; a single political shift can stop the project system-wide."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Overview — merchant–state alignment,"Merchant–state alignment: European expansion often blends state aims (power/war) with private profit motives → persistent pressure to expand."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Overview — security priorities,"Security priorities: a land-empire facing frontier threats may rationally reallocate resources away from long oceanic projects (your “pirates / emperor focus” logic fits here)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.1 Intro,Great Divergence link — Pomeranz + Wallerstein channels,"Great Divergence link: if Europe gains access to colonies/resources and builds naval/financial institutions, it strengthens the very channels that later help it “escape constraints” (Pomeranz) and enforce hierarchy (Wallerstein)."
