2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.2 Zheng He vs. Portuguese,Zheng He voyages — dates + destinations,"1405–1433 Zheng He to Oceania, India, Middle East, and Eastern Africa."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.2 Zheng He vs. Portuguese,Zheng He — ships/technology + distance,"Technology was huge ships not small ones; Eastern Africa already very far from home."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.2 Zheng He vs. Portuguese,Winds/navigation — Zheng He vs Portuguese asymmetry,"Navigating southwards difficult due to conditions of the wind; opposite situation compared to Portuguese: they had great difficulties at beginning of journey while Zheng He great difficulties at end of journey."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.2 Zheng He vs. Portuguese,Zheng He — capability but politically contingent,"Zheng He voyages show capability (state-organized, large-scale expeditions), but voyages are politically contingent (court priorities) and less embedded in private profit-seeking systems."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.2 Zheng He vs. Portuguese,Portuguese expansion — what characterizes it?,"Portuguese expansion is incremental learning (stepwise coastal exploration) tied to forts/enclaves and later to monopoly company logic; reinforced by interstate rivalry (Spain/Netherlands/England follow)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Why Japan is central — two reasons + later exception,"Japan is a natural comparison case because it shows both: the ability of non-European societies to participate in early modern trade/tech flows, and how domestic political objectives can lead to closure (and thus reduced long-run exposure to maritime competition/innovation pressures). Later, Japan becomes the key exception in Asia: Major Restoration (Meiji) → institutional reorganization → transition to industrial economy."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Initial contact — Portuguese entry as contingent event + firearms,"Portuguese in Japan by “random”: provide firearms for civil/Shogunate conflicts. Firearms become a political and military bargaining chip: access to new weapons shifts internal balance of power; creates incentives for local rulers to trade with Europeans (short-run gain)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Mechanism — “globalization as a shock” (Japan case),"This is an example of “globalization as a shock”: external technology enters, but the domestic political equilibrium determines whether the shock leads to openness, regulation, or closure."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Japan–China–Portugal triangle — restricted access → rents → violence → closure,"Where states restrict official trade, intermediation (and often coercion) expands: restricted access → creates rents → attracts armed traders/pirates → raises violence → increases incentives for stronger regulation/closure."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Micro-example link to world-system logic,"This is also a micro-example of how unequal bargaining power + armed commerce foreshadows later world-system hierarchy."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Tokugawa/Shogunate closure — what and why,"Shogunate decides to close itself wanting to end trouble until Major Restoration. After war/conflict done: ban Portuguese; replace by Dutch (more controlled channel). Closure is not “irrational backwardness”; it can be a rational strategy for domestic stability (reduce foreign influence + weapons diffusion), controlling trade rents through a narrow gatekeeper system, and suppressing internal challengers who might leverage foreign connections."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Comparative point — China/Japan vs Europe,"In both China and Japan, central political priorities can dominate economic opportunity: when the goal is stability/security, the state may sacrifice dynamic gains from openness. Europe differs because fragmentation makes sustained closure harder: if one ruler restricts, merchants and innovators can shift elsewhere."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Pomeranz framing — Japan pre-19th path and exposure to pressures,"In Pomeranz framing: advanced Eurasian regions face shared preindustrial constraints (land/energy, diminishing returns); escaping constraints requires a breakthrough path (energy substitution, access to resources, institutional reorganization). Japan’s pre-19th century path is consistent with managed/limited integration and then closure, which reduces exposure to large-scale oceanic competition and sustained pressure to innovate in naval and military-fiscal systems."

2. The Great Divergence,2.6 Why Europe, not China? (Comparative Maritime Expansion / Political Economy),2.6.3 Japan,Meiji/Major Restoration — bullets + lesson,"Japan Major Restoration: abolish Shogunate; only Asian country to transition to industrial economy (in your notes). Mechanism: regime change reorients the state toward catching up militarily and economically; enables adoption/implementation of industrial and institutional reforms. Lesson: institutional/political turning points can create late but rapid transitions; “not diverging early” doesn’t imply “incapable of diverging later.”"

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),World-System approach — guiding question,"How can a small initial advantage of some parts of Western Europe become self-reinforcing and produce a durable hierarchy in the world economy?"

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),World-System approach — core claim,"The World-System approach: Divergence is not only internal growth; it is also about how a global system gets structured (who controls trade, violence, rules) and how this structure creates persistent inequality."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Braudel — world economy vs world-economy (definition + quote),"Braudel: distinction between world economy vs world-economy. “The world economy is an expression applied to the whole world. (...) A world-economy only concerns a fragment of the world, an economically autonomous section.”"

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Braudel — implication for earlier chapters (2.3 → 2.4 → 2.5),"Before the Great Opening, there were multiple world-economies (Eurasia not one integrated economy). Empires (Roman, Mongol) create more coherence inside zones but do not automatically create one global economy."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Braudel — long durée + capitalism above markets,"Braudel often emphasizes the long durée (slow structures) and the idea that “capitalism” is not simply markets, but organized power + privileged actors operating above ordinary exchange."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Wallerstein 1 — before globalization: properties of separate world-systems/world-economies,"Wallerstein – World System Theory: Before globalization: separate world-systems/world-economies, each tied to a geographic space that is: interconnected; hierarchically organized; relatively self-sufficient; and almost entirely separated from others."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Wallerstein 1 — center/periphery,"Each has its own Center / Periphery (concept introduced early; detailed later)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Wallerstein 2 — formation of first global world-system (16th c. onwards): core/periphery/semi-periphery,"Creation of the first global “world system” as a progressive construction of an interrelated system comprising: Core: areas with concentrated power and organizational capacity (military + financial + political) that can shape rules and capture high returns. Periphery: areas incorporated on unequal terms, typically supplying land-/labor-intensive goods and/or coerced labor, with less bargaining power. Semi-periphery: intermediate areas (not fully dominant, not fully dominated) — useful category because it explains: why the system has “middle” zones and buffers; why hierarchy is not just two levels."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Wallerstein 2 — core mechanism statement,"Core mechanism: dominion of the centre (economic + political + military) imposes a system of inegalitarian exchanges which: consolidates the primacy of the centre itself; tends to make permanent the relative under-development of the peripheries."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.1 World-System approach (Braudel/Wallerstein),Wallerstein 2 — origin claim + mechanism chain,"Origin claim: a small advantage acquired early by parts of Western Europe becomes amplified and made durable by the expansion of the world-system. → control of routes + coercion/monopolies → ability to set terms of trade → surplus flows to core → core reinvests in navy/finance/state capacity → stronger enforcement → more unequal exchange."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.2 Pomeranz vs Wallerstein,Pomeranz critique — overweighting merchant trade as engine of capitalism,"Overweighting international merchants/trade as the engine of capitalism: Pomeranz: both Braudel and Wallerstein pay excessive attention to great merchants involved in international trade as key actors of “capitalism”."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.2 Pomeranz vs Wallerstein,Pomeranz — comparative claim about core areas (Europe vs Asia proto-growth),"International trade apart, market-driven growth in western Europe’s core areas in the early modern period was not more conducive to industrial transformation than the similar commercialization and proto-industrial growth occurring in several Asian core areas."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.2 Pomeranz vs Wallerstein,Pomeranz — implications (commercialization not sufficient; look at blocks),"Therefore: Europe’s earlier commercialization is not sufficient to explain the Industrial Revolution. You need to look at what blocked industrialization in both places, not only at trade hierarchy."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.2 Pomeranz vs Wallerstein,Pomeranz partial agreement — colonies/extraction matter,"Pomeranz agrees that (partial agreement): exploitation of non-Europeans; access to overseas resources; and “colonial extraction” played an important role."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.2 Pomeranz vs Wallerstein,Pomeranz key move — constraints + escape (Britain) not just trade,"Where Pomeranz fundamentally differs (not “trade vs no trade,” but “constraints + escape”): shift attention from “merchant capitalism” to constraints on industrialization: the decisive question is: what factors constrained the transition to industrialization in Europe and Asia? and what allowed Britain (not all Europe) to overcome them?"

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.2 Pomeranz vs Wallerstein,Pomeranz — colonies not sole engine but necessary context,"Colonies are not the sole engine: internal development processes occurred in western Europe and eastern Asia. But colonies are still necessary context: the “great transformation of the nineteenth century” could occur only in a context shaped by Europe’s privileged access to overseas resources; without those resources, western Europe would have been pushed toward more labor-intensive growth, as happened to China and Japan."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Pomeranz — starting point: shared core-region features across Eurasia,"Pomeranz’s starting point: across Eurasia, advanced preindustrial regions (W. Europe, China, Japan; possibly parts of India) shared broadly similar “core-region” features: dense populations; productive agriculture; extensive commerce; significant handicraft / proto-industrial activity."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Pomeranz — key structural limitation + diminishing returns,"Key structural limitation: limited substitutability of labor and capital for land (and biomass energy) in preindustrial economies. Law of diminishing returns: pushing more labor onto land-intensive production eventually lowers marginal returns."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Pomeranz — core problem (what’s hard to do simultaneously),"Core problem (Pomeranz): these limits make it difficult to simultaneously: expand population; raise per-capita consumption; and increase specialization outside agriculture."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Pomeranz — why markets alone not decisive,"Trade helps but cannot fully solve constraints by itself in a world still bound by land/biomass energy. Comparative framing: this is why “market economy” differences alone are not decisive: the binding issue is escaping the constraint set, not simply having markets."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Constraint module — dense population (constraint + opportunity),"Dense population (constraint + opportunity): By ~1800, W. Europe, China, Japan are among the most densely populated regions. Constraint: additional population growth strains food supply → requires breakthrough. Opportunity: dense labor markets make it easier for elites to give up bound labor (still get workers at profit-compatible wages). Free labor markets can raise allocative efficiency and market participation."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Constraint module — depletion of natural resources (land pressure),"Depletion of natural resources (land pressure): Intensification supports more people but at rising ecological cost: deforestation; hillside erosion; increased risk of food shortages. Europe had some crop-mix flexibility for calories, but not easily or quickly. England’s labor-saving but soil-depleting strategy created medium-run risk → “needed” a new path. Europe disadvantaged in fiber crops (flax/hemp/cotton constraints); 19th c. textile growth required imports."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Constraint module — fuel/wood scarcity (energy constraint),"Fuel/wood scarcity (energy constraint): Core regions face dwindling biomass fuel supplies (wood), with low price elasticity. By the 18th century: rising demand, shrinking forest acreage (converted to arable), limited yield improvements. Timber shortage becomes acute; example note: France fuel wood price +91% (1726–1789). Regions with coal/peat fare better (Britain/Belgium; Netherlands peat)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Constraint module — building materials / ship timber (strategic constraint),"Building materials / ship timber (strategic constraint): High-quality ship timber shortages constrain naval capacity and construction: Britain reserves suitable trees in New England colonies for navy; shifts shipbuilding to forested colonies; one-third of Britain’s merchant fleet built in American colonies (eve of American Revolution). China: rising costs of wood for seagoing ships (+700% 1550–1820)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Britain’s double boom — coal + colonies (two escape levers),"Pomeranz’s key claim: without coal + colonies, Britain faces disaster (ecological trap). Two escape levers: (1) Coal: a domestic, scalable substitute for land-based energy (wood/charcoal) → supports productivity increases. (2) Colonies / overseas resources: relieve land/energy constraints through imports of resource-intensive goods: early: cotton, sugar, guano, timber/naval stores, etc.; later: grain, meat, logs."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Britain’s double boom — additional channel (population pressure), "Overseas settlement absorbs population pressure → slows ecological decline at home."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Why Britain specifically?,"Many European states had colonies, but Britain had both: extensive overseas access and vast, relatively accessible coal reserves."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.3 Pomeranz: Shared Constraints + Britain’s Escape,Interpretive quote idea — what Atlantic trade matters for,"Atlantic trade matters less for “financial profits” and more for relieving what is truly scarce: land and energy. Overseas extraction should be compared with coal as the crucial “exit route” from Malthusian constraints."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.4 Pomeranz vs “European Miracle” tradition,European Miracle tradition — two typical claims Pomeranz targets,"Two typical claims Pomeranz targets: (1) Early, Europe-wide divergence: the whole of Western Europe pulls ahead of East Asia from the early modern period (on GDP per capita / development). (2) Colonies don’t matter much: “for the economic growth of the core, the periphery was peripheral” (O’Brien-style position)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.4 Pomeranz vs “European Miracle” tradition,Pomeranz against early, Europe-wide divergence,"Against early, Europe-wide divergence: divergence begins from a specific part of Europe (Britain), no earlier than mid-18th century (≈ ≥1750), not across all Western Europe from 1500."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.4 Pomeranz vs “European Miracle” tradition,Pomeranz against “periphery was peripheral”,"Against “periphery was peripheral”: overseas resources and colonial extraction matter as constraint relief (land/energy), not merely profits."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.4 Pomeranz vs “European Miracle” tradition,How measurement debate enters (what depends on what you measure),"The disagreement partly depends on what you measure: output per capita vs living standards; which regions represent “Europe” and “Asia” (core regions vs national averages); wages/urbanization/GDP reconstructions and their uncertainty."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.4 Pomeranz vs “European Miracle” tradition,O’Brien counterpoint + measurement limits,"O’Brien counterpoint: focus on standards of living; claim poverty less widespread in Europe (possible distribution channel) → measurement limits remain."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.4 Pomeranz vs “European Miracle” tradition,Pomeranz summary sentence,"Pomeranz rejects a “natural” European lead and argues that industrialization required a late, contingent escape from shared Eurasian constraints—made possible in Britain by the combined availability of coal and overseas resources."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Demography story — core Malthusian claim,"In a Malthusian economy, improvements in productivity tend to be offset by population growth → living standards revert toward subsistence. A persistent change in mortality / demographic regime can prevent population from fully absorbing productivity gains, allowing real wages and living standards to stay higher for longer."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Black Death shock — dates + mortality magnitude,"Shock: Black Death as a structural break (1347–1352): In 1347, plague came back to Europe. The ensuing Black Death killed 30–50% of Europe’s population."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Black Death — “positive” economic consequences list,"Many highlight “positive” economic consequences: rebalancing of population–resources ratio; more rational land use / higher land-labor ratio; upward pressure on wages (labor scarcity)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Clark’s mechanism — high-mortality regime matters (not one-off shock),"Gregory Clark (A Farewell to Alms): it is not only the one-off shock that matters, but mortality per se. Plague becomes endemic in Europe, creating a high-mortality demographic regime."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Clark mechanism chain (1–5),"Mechanism chain (Clark logic): (1) repeated plagues periodically reduce population (2) → prevents population growth from “destroying” per-capita gains from technical progress (3) → maintains higher land/labor ratio (4) → sustained increases in real wages (proxy for productivity and living standards) (5) → contributes to divergence between Western Europe and Eastern Asia (where this exact mortality regime is not assumed to operate in the same way)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Evidence — post-Black Death wages + why wages used,"Real wages rise after the Black Death in many European areas → consistent with labor scarcity and sustained higher living standards. Real wages are used as a proxy because: if real wages rise persistently, this suggests higher marginal product of labor / higher productivity (with caveats about representativeness)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Figures placement notes (Pamuk/Clark), "Pamuk (2007)-type multi-series chart on Black Death and the origin of divergence across Europe (1300–1600) → put right after 2.7.5.2 or at start of 2.7.5.4. Clark (2007)-type long-run real wage series (England 1209–1867) → put in 2.7.5.4 as the “canonical long-run wage evidence.”"

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Where Broadberry fits (“partly” demographic),"Broadberry is not “pure Clark,” but parts of Broadberry-style work align with demographic interpretations when: they emphasize earlier European advantage (pre-Industrial) in output/living standards in some regions, and/or they use wages / urbanization / GDP reconstructions consistent with an earlier divergence timing. In your structure, Broadberry connects here mainly through: the earliest-origins hypothesis (divergence mechanisms begin around/after Black Death), and the measurement debate (how wages/GDP/urbanization are reconstructed and compared across regions)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Limits/critiques (demographic story) — list,"From wages to industrialization is not automatic: high wages can exist without an industrial takeoff; the demographic story needs a bridge to technological/institutional changes. Europe is heterogeneous: plague intensity and wage paths vary across regions/cities; “Europe” is not one case. Comparability issues: wage baskets, worker types, rural vs urban, and selection of regions affect conclusions (“whose wage?”). Alternative mechanisms overlap: even if wages rise, you still need to explain why Britain (or parts of Europe) later industrialize (links to 2.7.3 Pomeranz; and to institutional accounts)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.5 Demography – Gregory Clark & partly Broadberry,Clark demographic story — one-sentence summary,"Clark’s demographic story explains early divergence by arguing that Europe’s plague-driven high-mortality regime repeatedly reduced population, keeping wages and living standards elevated by preventing Malthusian reversion—an interpretation supported by post-Black Death wage increases, though it doesn’t by itself explain the later British industrial breakthrough."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Diamond — core claim (what geography story explains),"Long-run divergence is driven by deep geographic and biogeographic differences that shape: where food production can arise early; how fast agriculture/technology diffuse; resulting population density, state capacity, and technological accumulation; and ultimately the ability to conquer (“guns, germs, steel”)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Diamond causal chain — ultimate causes → proximate causes,"Ultimate cause → proximate causes (Diamond’s causal chain): Ultimate causes: geography, domesticable plants/animals, continental orientation, ecological barriers. These generate proximate causes: early farming → food surplus → specialists → states/armies/technology; dense societies + domestic animals → exposure to zoonoses → immunity advantages. Result: by the time of transoceanic contact, Eurasian societies possess the cumulative “package” that matters for conquest."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Diamond starting puzzle — why food production emerges unevenly,"Question is not simply “fertile vs infertile,” but why some apparently suitable regions did not develop food production early."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Primary centers list (independent origins of food production),"“Primary centers” of independent origins of food production: Fertile Crescent / Southwest Asia; China; Mesoamerica; Andes (+ possibly adjacent Amazonia); Eastern United States."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Candidate/uncertain centers list,"Candidate/uncertain centers: Sahel; Tropical West Africa; Ethiopia; New Guinea."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Development pathways (adoption vs replacement),"Development pathways: adoption without replacement (gradual incorporation); abrupt replacement by incoming farmers."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Why adopt farming if early farming was worse? (Diamond Ch. 6) — framing,"Early farming often worsened life (nutrition, workload, disease exposure), so adoption is not “choosing progress.” Treat farming vs hunter-gathering as competing strategies; when farming exists nearby, groups can: adopt full-package farming; adopt piecemeal mixed strategies; reject and remain hunter-gatherers."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Five drivers of adoption (Diamond Ch. 6 list 1–5),"Five drivers of adoption: (1) decline of wild foods (2) increased availability of domesticable wild plants (3) cumulative technology for harvesting/processing/storage (4) positive feedback: population density ↔ food production intensification (5) competition/displacement: denser food producers outcompete/replace H&G."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Diffusion advantage — Eurasia east–west axis (Diamond Ch. 10),"Eurasia’s east–west orientation: similar latitude → similar climate/day length → easier diffusion of crops/animals and related technologies. Americas/Africa north–south orientation: big changes in climate/day length/disease environments → harder diffusion."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Diffusion spillovers,"Diffusion has spillovers: faster spread of agriculture bundles also accelerates spread of other innovations (e.g., wheel/alphabet in your notes)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Geopolitics add-on — fragmentation and competition (why Europe keeps innovating),"Natural barriers fragment Europe → favors many medium-size competing states. Competition increases incentives to innovate (military, naval, fiscal). In large empires (China/Japan), rulers can ban technologies if they threaten stability; in absence of direct rivals, the short-run cost of banning innovation is lower."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Examples of bans/abandonment (Japan/China) + Columbus funding logic,"Examples in your notes: Japanese abandonment of firearms (17th c.); Chinese abandonment of oceanic navigation after Zheng He voyages (1405–1433). Funding logic illustration: Columbus rejected by Portugal → can seek Spain; in a single empire system, rejection can end the project (no alternative sponsor)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Link to conquest — disease immunity + package,"Eurasian advantage in domestic animals and dense farming societies → greater disease exposure → immunity → decisive in “virgin soil” encounters. Combined with technological and state capacity differences, this helps explain why the Americas’ most advanced states were vulnerable at contact. (Links back to your 2.4.7–2.4.9 conquest/biological exchange notes.)"

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Limits/critiques in Great Divergence debates,"Diamond is strongest on very long-run divergence (Eurasia vs rest), but less precise on: the timing and location of the 18th–19th century break (Britain vs Yangzi Delta, etc.). Risks being too structural to explain shorter-run institutional choices and reversals (e.g., why some regions industrialize first). Still complements other accounts: provides “starting conditions,” while Pomeranz/Needham/Clark target the mechanisms of the later break."

2. The Great Divergence,2.7 Competing Causal Stories,2.7.6 Geography/Geopolitcs – Diamond,Diamond — final summary sentence,"Diamond explains divergence through deep geography and biogeography that shape the early rise and diffusion of agriculture, population density, disease immunity, and state capacity—creating cumulative Eurasian advantages in “guns, germs, and steel,” reinforced in Europe by political fragmentation and competition."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.1 Why measurement is not “neutral”,Why measurement is not “neutral” — core claim,"The Great Divergence debate is partly a debate about what counts as development and which indicator is credible for preindustrial societies."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.1 Why measurement is not “neutral”,Why measurement is not “neutral” — what indicators are sensitive to,"Different indicators are sensitive to: unit of analysis (country averages vs leading regions/cities); representativeness (urban vs rural; skilled vs unskilled); prices/baskets (what is a “subsistence basket”?); institutions and distribution (GDP can rise while many stay poor)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,Urbanization rates — logic,"Logic: urbanization ↔ non-agricultural employment ↔ agricultural surplus above subsistence → proxy for long-term structural development."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,Urbanization rates — strength,"Strength: available for long horizons; captures surplus capacity."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,Urbanization rates — weaknesses,"Weakness: cities can be administrative/military rather than “productive”; different urban definitions across regions; does not directly measure living standards or inequality."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,GDP per capita reconstructions — logic,"Logic: direct measure of output per person; allows “levels” comparisons."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,GDP per capita reconstructions — strength,"Strength: speaks directly to “divergence in income.”"

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,GDP per capita reconstructions — weaknesses,"Weakness: high uncertainty, sparse data, assumptions heavy; country averages can hide “core-region” parity (Pomeranz point); does not automatically measure welfare (prices, distribution, household production)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,Real wages — logic,"Logic: wage ≈ marginal product of labor; rising real wages suggest higher productivity/living standards."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,Real wages — strength,"Strength: close to living standards; good for timing breaks (e.g., post-Black Death; post-industrial wage takeoff)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.2 Main indicators and what they measure,Real wages — weaknesses,"Weakness: “whose wages?” (urban/rural, skill, gender, occupation); wages reflect bargaining institutions and labor market structure, not only productivity; basket construction and price comparability are decisive."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Demography — prefers what measures?,"Demography (Clark; partly Broadberry) prefers: real wages + timing around demographic shocks."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Demography — prediction pattern,"Prediction pattern: Black Death → wage increase in Europe (labor scarcity); persistence depends on mortality regime (endemic plague preventing Malthusian reversion)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Demography — measurement risks,"Measurement risk: using a few European wage series as “Europe”; comparing urban wages to rural Asia (selection bias)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Pomeranz — prefers what evidence?,"Pomeranz (constraints + escape) prefers: indicators of ecological/energy constraints + evidence of “escape” (wood/fuel scarcity, land constraints, resource prices; evidence of coal substitution and overseas resource relief (“virtual land/energy”))."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Pomeranz — unit of comparison insistence,"Uses wages/GDP as outcomes, but insists on comparing leading regions (Yangzi Delta vs England), not national averages."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Pomeranz — measurement risks,"Measurement risk: if you use country GDP averages, you may “manufacture” early divergence; if you only use selected core regions, you may understate within-region poverty/distribution differences."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,World-system — prefers what evidence?,"World-system (Braudel/Wallerstein) prefers: evidence of hierarchy and unequal exchange (trade route control, monopoly institutions, coercion, colonial specialization patterns; shifts in terms of trade and enforcement capacity — “if you rebel… intervention”)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,World-system — what trade integration is used for,"Uses trade integration as proxy for system formation (“trade connections as proxy/estimand”)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,World-system — measurement risks,"Measurement risk: trade volume can be tiny but system impact large (your Chaunu point); correlation vs causation (does hierarchy cause underdevelopment or reflect it?)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Diamond — prefers what indicators?,"Diamond (deep geography) prefers: very long-run indicators of food production and diffusion and state capacity (agricultural origins, axis effects, domesticable packages, disease environment)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Diamond — measurement risk,"Measurement risk: strongest at Eurasia vs rest; less precise for Britain vs China timing."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Institutional — prefers what proxies?,"Institutional (Needham/van Zanden/Broadberry etc.) prefers: proxies for market integration, state capacity, and innovation infrastructure (urbanization, finance/credit institutions, chartered towns, universities, constraints on rulers)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.3 How each causal story “wants” you to measure divergence,Institutional — measurement risk,"Measurement risk: institutions are difficult to quantify; danger of “selecting on the outcome.”"

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.4 Why the “start date” debate is partly a measurement debate,Start date debate — earliest origins and typical evidence,"Earliest origins (post-1347): often supported by wage dynamics (Clark-type evidence)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.4 Why the “start date” debate is partly a measurement debate,Start date debate — early origins and typical evidence,"Early origins (1500–1700): often supported by commercialization/urbanization and institutional narratives."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.4 Why the “start date” debate is partly a measurement debate,Start date debate — late origins and typical evidence,"Late origins (≥1750): often supported by takeoff patterns in wages/output and the coal/colonies escape logic."

2. The Great Divergence,2.8 Measurement debate revised (How to tie evidence to Theories?),2.8.5 Practical rule for your notes (triangulation),Triangulation rule (1–4),"To avoid getting trapped in one indicator: (1) Use at least two development measures (e.g., wages + urbanization or wages + GDP reconstructions). (2) Always specify the unit of comparison (country vs core region vs city). (3) Distinguish output (GDP) from living standards (wages/consumption) and from power (state capacity / coercion). (4) Treat maps/colonies/trade networks as system structure evidence, not direct welfare evidence."

2. The Great Divergence,2.9 Long-Run Persistence,2.9.1 European Colonial Empires 1880–1914,European colonial empires 1880–1914 — peak reach and what it illustrates,"Late 19th / early 20th century marks the peak territorial reach of European imperial power."

2. The Great Divergence,2.9 Long-Run Persistence,2.9.1 European Colonial Empires 1880–1914,Why it matters for earlier sections (3 points),"Illustrates the mature form of core power projection (naval/military/fiscal capacity); shows how the global economy can be structured through political control, not only markets; reinforces “enforcement clause”: hierarchy persists because it can be enforced."

2. The Great Divergence,2.9 Long-Run Persistence,2.9.2 World System late 20th Century,World-system late 20th century — persistence after decolonization,"Even after decolonization, global inequality can remain structured in core / semi-periphery / periphery patterns (world-system vocabulary)."

2. The Great Divergence,2.9 Long-Run Persistence,2.9.2 World System late 20th Century,What this adds conceptually — persistence channels,"Hierarchy can persist via: trade specialization patterns; capital/technology concentration; geopolitical influence; institutional and path-dependent legacies; shows why “globalization” does not automatically imply “convergence.”"

2. The Great Divergence,2.9 Long-Run Persistence,2.9.2 World System late 20th Century,Interpretive caution,"This map is a descriptive classification (based on trade/status categories), not a causal test. Use it to illustrate persistence of structure, then point back to 2.7 debates for causal interpretation."
